title: Evenings with Graham & Emmy
teaser: Garfield Twp advances moratorium while Saline hits $16B and more pauses emerge.

lines.text: Good evening and welcome to Evenings with Graham & Emmy, the Michigan Data Wire, your podcast on Michigan's data center buildout. I'm Graham.
And I'm Emmy.
Tonight we're digging into why one northwest Michigan township just backed a pause on new data centers and what that signals for communities statewide.
Emmy, the big story today comes out of Garfield Township in Grand Traverse County.
Right, their planning commission advanced a moratorium proposal while residents hash out how these big facilities hit local power and land use.
Exactly, and this isn't happening in isolation.
No, we've seen similar moves in other spots recently.
Let's start with the facts on Garfield. The panel recommended freezing new applications so folks can debate the impacts properly.
Why does this matter so much right now? Data center interest is growing fast in northwest Michigan.
It is, and residents there are worried about sudden spikes in electricity demand and what that does to their bills.
Plus the land itself. These projects take up a lot of space that could stay farmland or open space.
That's the core tension. Townships are trying to hit pause before decisions get locked in.
How long have these conversations been building? It feels like more than just one township reacting.
It has been rolling for weeks now. Yesterday Haring Township in Wexford County set up a vote on a full six-month moratorium to block new applications while they write permanent rules.
And they want time to study water use and tax impacts first.
Right, same concerns about noise from hyperscale facilities and power strain.
Solon Township also shelved rules that would have limited where these big places can go.
They delayed the ordinance, so the status quo stays while officials figure out next steps on roads and power.
That uncertainty is tough for neighbors who just want clarity.
It is. And this pattern shows up in multiple counties now.
So what's driving all these pauses at once?
Mainly the scale of what's proposed. Look at Saline Township today. Their project just locked in financing and swelled to sixteen billion dollars.
Sixteen billion? That rivals the biggest campuses in the country.
It does, and it will shape Washtenaw County growth for years, including any tax incentives that hit local taxpayers.
How does that connect to electric bills for regular folks?

A Monroe utility analyst warned yesterday that unchecked growth means new power plants and transmission upgrades, and those costs flow straight to residential ratepayers through DTE and Consumers Energy.
So the bigger the project, the bigger the potential hit on monthly bills.
That's the fear, and it's why ratepayers are rallying next week with activists from more than fifty communities.
The July eighteenth event in Kent County is highlighting exactly those power and water demands from AI facilities.
Yes, and it's showing the debate is moving beyond township halls into the streets.
Water is another big piece, right? These facilities use a lot for cooling.
They do, and Haring residents specifically called that out along with noise when they pushed for their moratorium.
Farmland loss is the other side of the land-use coin.
In Ingham County, Mason residents are already accusing outsiders of faking grassroots support in their local fight, and they're worried about exactly that, plus noise and power strain.
The trust issues there sound pretty raw.
They are, and it's fracturing the conversation just as officials weigh next steps.
Meanwhile, the political side is lining up too.

Yesterday all four Republican candidates for governor said they support tax incentives during a Grand Rapids debate, arguing the projects bring construction jobs and long-term property tax revenue to rural townships.
They didn't get into the details on household electric rates or water supplies though.
Not much detail there, which leaves voters to fill in the gaps.
And on the federal side, Rep. Debbie Dingell is probing a University of Michigan data center plan with Los Alamos ties.
She released letters raising concerns about power demand, water use, and university involvement.
That adds another layer for communities near potential sites.
It does. And the carpenters union threw support behind the STREAM project yesterday, signaling hundreds of construction jobs if it gets approved.
Steady paychecks for local workers, but new demands on roads, water, and electricity for nearby towns.
That's the trade-off communities keep coming back to.
So where does Garfield go from here? The planners backed the freeze option, but what happens next?
Township leaders still need to adopt the recommendation before any pause takes effect, and residents will keep debating the power and land impacts.
It gives everyone a window to look at the bigger picture.
Exactly, and with similar pauses popping up elsewhere, the pattern suggests more townships may follow suit while they study the numbers.
What about the scale of these facilities overall? A gigawatt-scale campus is enormous.
It is, and when you stack that against existing grid capacity, the upgrades needed become a statewide conversation.
Especially with multiple projects eyeing different regions at once.
Right, from northwest Michigan down to Washtenaw and Ingham Counties.
So the moratorium trend might be the first real pushback we're seeing.
It looks that way today, and it will be interesting to watch how many more townships pick up the same approach.
Listeners should keep an eye on those upcoming votes.
Let's shift to a few quick hits from the last day or so.
Saline Township locked in that sixteen-billion-dollar financing today, so the project is moving forward with major tax incentive questions still on the table.
And Solon Township delayed its restrictive ordinance, leaving the status quo in place for now.
Haring Township is voting tomorrow on its six-month moratorium to study water and power impacts.
Carpenters union endorsement for STREAM came yesterday, highlighting the job side of the ledger.
Dingell's letters on the U-M and Los Alamos plan also surfaced yesterday, focusing on infrastructure strain.
Ratepayers are rallying July eighteenth across more than fifty communities worried about AI power and water demands.
Mason residents are pushing back on what they call fake grassroots support in their local debate.
And all four GOP governor candidates backed tax breaks in yesterday's Grand Rapids debate.
Now let's look ahead to meetings listeners can actually attend.
Tomorrow, July thirteenth, Lansing City Council Development and Planning holds a public hearing on a one-hundred-eighty-two-day data center moratorium.

And on July fourteenth, Saline Township Board of Trustees will vote on a twelve-year, fifty-percent tax abatement for the Oracle America Cloud Services project tied to the Related Digital AI data center, plus a public hearing on the Industrial Facilities Exemption Certificate.
Plenty for folks to weigh in on if they're in those areas.
Full sources and the live map at the Michigan Data Center Tracker.
We're back tomorrow.

episode_title: Garfield Township Eyes Data Center Freeze as Power, Water and Farmland Pressures Build Acr
